title: Anthropic Just Dropped a Masterclass on Building Agent Harnesses (for Large Codebases)
summary: Cole Medin presents an AI Layer harness architecture for Claude Code in large codebases, demonstrating that the surrounding tooling and context management exceeds model capability alone. The harness includes CLAUDE.md hierarchy, self-improving hooks, scoped skills, language server protocol integration, model context protocol, subagents, and a bundling plugin.
writeup: This tutorial translates Anthropic's blog post on using Claude Code in large codebases into concrete, demonstrated strategies, centering on the claim that the harness matters as much as the model. Claude Code navigates codebases through agentic search (grep and folder traversal, not RAG or indexing), which means it performs best when given enough upfront context to know where to look. The video walks through seven components of an "AI layer" added to any codebase, from lean, layered CLAUDE.md files to hooks and MCP servers, each with a live demo in a purpose-built example repository.

key_points: Claude Code uses agentic search rather than traditional RAG or semantic indexing: it navigates a codebase the way an engineer would, using command-line tools like grep and reading directory structure, with no index to keep in sync but a corresponding dependency on good upfront context.

Anthropic's core thesis is that the harness matters as much as the model: seven components form the AI layer around a codebase (global rules, skills, MCP servers, sub-agents, LSP, hooks, and a codebase map), and tuning these outweighs chasing model benchmark improvements.

Global rules (CLAUDE.md) must be kept lean rather than exhaustive: studies show that thousands of lines of rules hurt coding agent performance, and the file should contain only the tech stack overview, key conventions, and essential commands.

CLAUDE.md files can be layered across subdirectories and are loaded progressively as the agent works deeper into the tree, so subdirectory-specific conventions load only when relevant rather than bloating every session's context.

Initializing Claude Code in a subdirectory rather than the repo root scopes its working directory and its loaded conventions to the relevant service or module, a simple navigational choice that avoids context contamination across unrelated parts of a large monorepo.
Hooks can be used to make the AI layer self-improving, automatically updating rules or other layer components based on what the agent encounters during a session.
